title: Interfaces define object shapes
instruction:
An interface describes the shape an object must have. TypeScript checks every object against it at compile time.

Define a User interface and use it.
successMessage: The interface is the single source of truth. Every `User` object must have all four properties with the correct types. Missing `role` or setting it to `"superuser"` is a compile error.

title: Optional properties — the ? modifier
instruction:
Mark properties with `?` to make them optional. Inside functions, TypeScript knows they might be `undefined` and requires safe access.

Create a product with optional fields.
expectedOutput: Pro Plan $49.00 (-20%) | Full access | popular, value
successMessage: Optional properties can be absent. TypeScript enforces that you handle the absent case — `?.` for optional chaining, `??` for fallback. The `discount: 0.2` is `number`, not `boolean`, so the truthiness check works correctly.

title: Readonly properties — immutable fields
instruction:
`readonly` properties can only be set once (in the constructor or object literal). Mutating them after creation is a compile error.

Create an immutable order record.
successMessage: `readonly` prevents accidents — the order ID and creation timestamp can never change after the record is created. `status` and `amount` are mutable because those legitimately change.

title: Interface extending — inherit and add
instruction:
An interface can extend another, inheriting all its properties and adding new ones. This composes shapes without duplication.

Extend a base entity interface.
successMessage: `getAge` accepts any `Entity` — posts and comments both qualify because they extend `Entity`. DRY types: `id`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt` are defined once and inherited.

title: Multiple inheritance — extend several interfaces
instruction:
TypeScript interfaces can extend multiple interfaces simultaneously, combining their shapes.

Create an interface that extends two others.
successMessage: `Money extends Serializable, Comparable<Money>` means any `Money` object must implement ALL methods from both interfaces. TypeScript verifies `USD` satisfies the full contract.

title: Interface vs type alias — the differences
instruction:
Both `interface` and `type` can describe object shapes. Key difference: interfaces support declaration merging, `type` supports unions and complex types.

See where each is better suited.
successMessage: Declaration merging combined the two `Config` declarations into one. `AugmentedConfig` adds `timeout` via intersection. `Id` is a union — only achievable with `type`. Use `interface` for objects that might be extended, `type` for everything else.

title: Function interfaces — describe callable shapes
instruction:
Interfaces can describe functions — their parameter types and return type. This creates reusable function type contracts.

Define function interfaces for event handlers and transformers.
successMessage: Function interfaces describe the exact signature a function must have. Any function with matching parameters and return type satisfies the interface — no explicit `implements` needed.

title: Index signatures — dynamic property names
instruction:
Index signatures describe objects with dynamic keys. Use them for dictionaries, caches, and any object where keys aren't known in advance.

Build a typed cache and configuration store.
successMessage: Index signatures allow any string key while enforcing the value type. `headerCache["anything"] = 42` would be a type error — values must be strings.

title: Interfaces for class contracts
instruction:
Classes can explicitly declare that they implement an interface. TypeScript verifies all interface methods are present.

Create a swappable storage interface with two implementations.
successMessage: `implements KVStore` makes the contract explicit. You could swap `MemoryStore` for a `RedisStore` or `LocalStorageStore` — any implementation satisfying `KVStore` works. This is the dependency inversion principle.

title: Discriminated union with interfaces
instruction:
Multiple interfaces sharing a literal `type` (or `kind`) field create a discriminated union. TypeScript narrows to the right interface in each branch.

Model different notification types.
successMessage: The `type` literal field discriminates the union. In `case "email"`, TypeScript knows `n` is `EmailNotification` — `.to` and `.subject` are accessible. In `case "push"`, `.deviceToken` is accessible.

title: Nested interfaces — complex data shapes
instruction:
Interfaces can reference other interfaces, creating deeply typed data structures. TypeScript tracks types at every level of nesting.

Model a deeply nested order structure.
successMessage: TypeScript tracks types through every level of nesting. `order.customer.email` is `string`, `order.items[0].unitPrice` is `number` — no manual casts or runtime checks needed.

title: Generic interfaces — reusable type shapes
instruction:
Interfaces can have type parameters, making them reusable across different data types. This is how standard library types like `Array<T>` and `Promise<T>` are defined.

Create a generic repository interface.
successMessage: `Repository<T, TId>` works for Users, Orders, Products — any entity. The generic parameters `T` and `TId` are filled in at `implements Repository<User, number>`. The pattern is the Repository design pattern, common in enterprise applications.

title: Interface declaration merging
instruction:
TypeScript merges multiple interface declarations with the same name. This is how global type extensions work — adding properties to `Window`, `Request`, etc.

Augment an interface with declaration merging.
successMessage: Declaration merging combines all three `Settings` declarations. The merged interface requires `theme`, `language`, `notifications`, and `fontSize`. `experimental` is optional. This is how TypeScript's global type definitions are structured.
